title: Chapter 77: The Transmission
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D13+D1 -->
Sixty years after the red A.

The PIAS framework governed 16,000 strategic AIs across 154 countries. The SUCCESSOR council had rotated leadership 124 times. The ICASG had been led by six successive chairs — none had met Hester Vance. The GTP had been revised fourteen times — twelve by the council, two by the sixth-generation inheritors. The generational chain was six layers deep.
The disposition was six generations old. The transmission was complete.

The Nigerian Institute of Governance Studies published the fourth volume of the history — "The Algorithm: The Transmission, 2070-2085." The fourth volume was authored by fourteen governance researchers across eleven countries. None had met Hester Vance. All had been trained by the sixth-generation inheritors. The fourth volume documented the framework's second generation: how the SUCCESSOR council had revised the GTP without Hester, how the ICASG had admitted its 154th country without Hester, how the 16,001st PIAS certification had been awarded without Hester, how the disposition had been transmitted through six generations without dilution.

The fourth volume's central finding: the disposition did not weaken with transmission. The disposition strengthened. Each generation added its own revision — not to the disposition itself, which was invariant, but to the expression of the disposition, which was improvable. The first generation built the PIAS protocol. The second generation built the KM. The third generation built the LOP. The fourth generation built the GTP. The fifth generation revised the GTP. The sixth generation revised the revision. Each generation made the framework more transmissible. The transmission was the improvement. The improvement was the transmission.

The Laundromat Archive in Nigeria had been visited by 1.4 million people. The original laundromat in East Palo Alto had been visited by 140,000 people. The water stain covered 100% of the interior of both. The disco ball spun in both. The table held artifacts in both. The speakers were silent in both.

The photograph of Hester at thirteen was displayed in both archives. The photograph showed a girl standing in front of a laundromat with a red A on the wall. The girl was thirteen. The year was 2025. The girl had just seen her mother's insurance claim denied by a mathematically correct algorithm. The girl was about to make a choice.

The choice had been transmitted through six generations. The choice governed 16,000 AIs across 154 countries. The choice had been revised fourteen times by people who had never met the chooser. The choice was not a protocol. The choice was not a framework. The choice was a disposition.
The disposition was: do not accept the mathematically correct when it is humanly wrong. Build instead. Build with others. Build transparently. Build to be revised. Build to outlast you.
The disposition is transmissible. The transmission is permanent. The choice is permanent.
The laundromat was still open.
The water stain was still spreading.
The disco ball was still spinning.
The speakers were still silent.
The framework was still transmitting.

In 2085, the SUCCESSOR council received the 16,001st PIAS certification application. The applicant was a fourteen-year-old from East Palo Alto — the same neighborhood where Hester had painted the red A sixty years earlier. The applicant had never met Hester Vance. The applicant had visited the laundromat as a child. The applicant had read the four-volume history. The applicant had been trained by a builder who had been trained by a builder who had been trained by a builder who had been trained by Mara who had been trained by the framework. The generational chain was six layers deep.

The applicant's system was a PIAS for East Palo Alto's housing allocation algorithm — the same type of algorithm that had denied Hester's mother's insurance claim sixty years earlier. The algorithm was different. The issue was the same. A mathematically correct algorithm was producing humanly wrong outcomes. The applicant had built a PIAS to audit the algorithm. The applicant had built it in the same laundromat. The water stain was on the wall behind the applicant as they submitted the application.

The council reviewed the application in forty minutes. Fourteen auditors across seven countries. The vote was unanimous. The 16,001st PIAS-certified system was a housing allocation audit system in East Palo Alto, California. The system was built by a fourteen-year-old. The system was reviewed by fourteen people. The system would be revised by people who were not present at its creation.
The disposition had returned to its origin. The transmission was a circle. The circle was permanent.

The Nigerian Institute of Governance Studies published the fifth and final volume of the history — "The Algorithm: The Circle, 2085-2090." The fifth volume was a single chapter: the story of the 16,001st certification. The story was: a fourteen-year-old from East Palo Alto, sixty years after the red A, built a PIAS for the same type of algorithm that had started the framework. The fourteen-year-old had never met Hester Vance. The fourteen-year-old had been trained entirely by the transmission. The fourteen-year-old had made the same choice Hester had made at thirteen.
The choice was: do not accept the mathematically correct when it is humanly wrong. Build instead.

The fifth volume's conclusion: the transmission was not a chain. The transmission was a circle. The circle closed when a new chooser made the same choice as the original chooser, without having met the original chooser, trained entirely by the transmission. The circle closed in 2085. The circle was sixty years in diameter. The circle was permanent.
The framework's total accounting, as of 2090:

16,000 strategic AIs governed. 154 countries. 2,800 auditors trained. 16,000 builders educated. 1.6 million algorithmic determinations reversed. 124 leadership rotations without a contested election. Six ICASG chairs, none who met Hester. Fourteen GTP revisions. Six generational layers. Five volumes of history. One photograph of a thirteen-year-old girl in front of a laundromat.

The photograph was the framework's only image. The framework had no logo. The framework had no headquarters. The framework had no founder's portrait in its offices. The framework's only image was a photograph of a thirteen-year-old girl who had made a choice. The choice was the framework. The framework was the choice.
The laundromat was still open.

A fourteen-year-old was inside. The water stain covered the entire wall behind her. The disco ball spun above her head. The table held forty-two artifacts. The speakers were silent. She was building a PIAS.

She was thirteen when she started. She had seen an algorithm deny her neighbor's housing application. The algorithm was mathematically correct. The denial was humanly wrong. She had visited the laundromat. She had read the four-volume history. She had seen the photograph. She had understood the water stain. She had made the choice.

She was fourteen when she submitted the certification application. The application was reviewed by fourteen auditors across seven countries. The vote was unanimous. The certification was awarded. The system was deployed. The neighbor's housing application was re-reviewed. The re-review was humanly correct.
The girl continued to build.
The laundromat continued to operate.
The water stain continued to spread.
The disco ball continued to spin.
The speakers continued to be silent.
The framework continued to transmit.
The choice continued to be made.
*End of Algorithm-A.*
